by [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") under [George II of Great Britain](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") on the grounds of [Trinity Church](/wiki/Trinity_Church_(Manhattan) "Trinity Church (Manhattan)") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), it is the oldest institution of higher education in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and the fifth-[oldest in the United States](/wiki/First_university_in_the_United_States "First university in the United States").

**Columbia University in the City of New York**,[[7]](#cite_note-macauhey-stand-Columbia-177-7) commonly referred to as **Columbia University**, is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), United States. Established in 1754 as **King's College** by [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") under [George II of Great Britain](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") on the grounds of [Trinity Church](/wiki/Trinity_Church_(Manhattan) "Trinity Church (Manhattan)") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), it is the oldest institution of higher education in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and the fifth-[oldest in the United States](/wiki/First_university_in_the_United_States "First university in the United States").

The university was renamed [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College_(New_York) "Columbia College (New York)") in 1784 following the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), and in 1787 was placed under [a private board of trustees](/wiki/Trustees_of_Columbia_University_in_the_City_of_New_York "Trustees of Columbia University in the City of New York") headed by former students [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") and [John Jay](/wiki/John_Jay "John Jay"). In 1896, the campus was moved to its current location and renamed Columbia University.

Columbia is organized into 17 schools, two affiliated colleges—[Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College") and [Teachers College](/wiki/Teachers_College,_Columbia_University "Teachers College, Columbia University")—and two affiliated seminaries—[Jewish Theological Seminary](/wiki/Jewish_Theological_Seminary_of_America "Jewish Theological Seminary of America") (JTS) and [Union Theological Seminary](/wiki/Union_Theological_Seminary "Union Theological Seminary") (UTS).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The university's research efforts include the [Lamont–Doherty Earth Observatory](/wiki/Lamont%E2%80%93Doherty_Earth_Observatory "Lamont–Doherty Earth Observatory"), the [Goddard Institute for Space Studies](/wiki/Goddard_Institute_for_Space_Studies "Goddard Institute for Space Studies"), and accelerator laboratories with [Big Tech](/wiki/Big_Tech "Big Tech") firms such as [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") and [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Columbia is a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") and was the first school in the United States to grant the [MD degree](/wiki/Doctor_of_Medicine "Doctor of Medicine").[[12]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Columbia2-12) The university also supports the administration of the annual [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), following the independent Pulitzer Prize Board's decisions.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Columbia scientists and scholars have played a pivotal role in scientific breakthroughs including [brain–computer interface](/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface "Brain–computer interface"); the [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") and [maser](/wiki/Maser "Maser");[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) [nuclear magnetic resonance](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance "Nuclear magnetic resonance");[[16]](#cite_note-16) the first [nuclear pile](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor"); the first [nuclear fission](/wiki/Nuclear_fission "Nuclear fission") reaction in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"); the first evidence for [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics") and [continental drift](/wiki/Continental_drift "Continental drift");[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) and much of the initial research and planning for the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

As of December 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Columbia_University&action=edit), its alumni, faculty, and staff have included 7 of the [Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States") of the United States of America;[[a]](#cite_note-27) [4 U.S. presidents](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States_by_education "List of presidents of the United States by education");[[b]](#cite_note-28) 34 foreign [heads of state or government](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state");[[c]](#cite_note-62) 2 secretaries-general of the United Nations;[[n 1]](#cite_note-64) 10 justices of the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"); [103 Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_affiliated_with_Columbia_University_as_alumni_or_faculty "List of Nobel laureates affiliated with Columbia University as alumni or faculty"); 125 [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") members;[[61]](#cite_note-65) 53 living billionaires;[[62]](#cite_note-CNBC2-66) [23 Olympic medalists](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals");[[63]](#cite_note-67) 33 [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") winners; and 125 Pulitzer Prize recipients.

## History

Main article: [History of Columbia University](/wiki/History_of_Columbia_University "History of Columbia University")

### 18th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Johnson2.JPG/250px-Johnson2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Johnson2.JPG)

[Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson_(American_educator) "Samuel Johnson (American educator)"), the first [president of Columbia](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_Columbia_University "List of presidents of Columbia University")

Discussions regarding the founding of a college in the [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") began as early as 1704.[[64]](#cite_note-McCaughey20032-68)[[65]](#cite_note-69)

Classes were initially held in July 1754 and were presided over by the college's first president, [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson_(American_educator) "Samuel Johnson (American educator)").[[66]](#cite_note-Matthews_19042-70): 8–10[[67]](#cite_note-Butler_19122-71): 3 The college was officially founded on October 31, 1754, as King's College by royal charter of [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"),[[68]](#cite_note-72)[[69]](#cite_note-73) making it the oldest institution of higher learning in the [State of New York](/wiki/State_of_New_York "State of New York") and the fifth oldest in the United States.[[12]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Columbia2-12)

In 1763, Johnson was succeeded in the presidency by [Myles Cooper](/wiki/Myles_Cooper "Myles Cooper"), a graduate of [The Queen's College, Oxford](/wiki/The_Queen%27s_College,_Oxford "The Queen's College, Oxford"), and an ardent [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)"). In the charged political climate of the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), his chief opponent in discussions at the college was an undergraduate of the class of 1777, [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton").[[67]](#cite_note-Butler_19122-71): 3 The [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") anatomist, [Samuel Clossy](/wiki/Samuel_Clossy "Samuel Clossy"), was appointed professor of natural philosophy in October 1765 and later the college's first professor of anatomy in 1767.[[70]](#cite_note-Stookey2-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Columbia1790.jpg/250px-Columbia1790.jpg)](/wiki/File:Columbia1790.jpg)

King's College Hall in 1790

Following the start of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") and the arrival of [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") in 1776, the school suspended instruction for eight years. The suspension continued through the military occupation of New York City by British troops until their [departure](/wiki/Evacuation_Day_(New_York) "Evacuation Day (New York)") in 1783. During this time, the college's library was looted and its sole building requisitioned for use as a military hospital first by American and then British forces.[[71]](#cite_note-75)[[72]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Columbia1797.jpg/250px-Columbia1797.jpg)](/wiki/File:Columbia1797.jpg)

The 1797 [Taylor Map](/wiki/Cartography_of_New_York_City "Cartography of New York City") of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), showing "The Colledge [sic]" at its Park Place (then Robinson Street) location and its earlier location, [Trinity Church](/wiki/Trinity_Church_(Manhattan) "Trinity Church (Manhattan)"), on the lower left

The legislature agreed to assist the college. On May 1, 1784, it passed "an Act for granting certain privileges to the College heretofore called King's College".[[66]](#cite_note-Matthews_19042-70) The Act created [a board of regents](/wiki/Board_of_Regents_of_the_University_of_the_State_of_New_York "Board of Regents of the University of the State of New York") to oversee the resuscitation of King's College. In an effort to demonstrate its support for the new Republic, the legislature stipulated that "the College within the City of New York heretofore called King's College be forever hereafter called and known by the name of [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College_of_Columbia_University "Columbia College of Columbia University")."[[66]](#cite_note-Matthews_19042-70) In February 1787, the Regents appointed a revision committee, which was headed by [John Jay](/wiki/John_Jay "John Jay") and Alexander Hamilton. In April of that same year, a new charter was adopted for the college granted the power to [a separate board of 24 trustees](/wiki/Trustees_of_Columbia_University_in_the_City_of_New_York "Trustees of Columbia University in the City of New York").[[73]](#cite_note-Moore-1846-77): 65–70

For a period in the 1790s, with New York City as the federal and state capital and the country under successive [Federalist](/wiki/Federalist_Party_(United_States) "Federalist Party (United States)") governments, a revived Columbia operated under Federalists such as Hamilton and Jay. President [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") and Vice President [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), in addition to both houses of [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") attended [the college's commencement](/wiki/Columbia_University_commencement "Columbia University commencement") on May 6, 1789, as a tribute of honor to the alumni of the school who had been involved in the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[66]](#cite_note-Matthews_19042-70): 74

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Columbia_law_madison.gif/250px-Columbia_law_madison.gif)](/wiki/File:Columbia_law_madison.gif)

The library and law school buildings, both constructed in a [Gothic and Revival](/w/index.php?title=Gothic_and_Revival_architecture&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gothic and Revival architecture (page does not exist)") style, on the [Madison Avenue](/wiki/Madison_Avenue "Madison Avenue") campus

### 19th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Detroit_Photographic_Company_%280671%29.jpg/250px-Detroit_Photographic_Company_%280671%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_Photographic_Company_(0671).jpg)

[Low Memorial Library](/wiki/Low_Memorial_Library "Low Memorial Library"), c. 1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Almamater.jpg/250px-Almamater.jpg)](/wiki/File:Almamater.jpg)

*[Alma Mater](/wiki/Alma_Mater_(New_York_sculpture) "Alma Mater (New York sculpture)")*, by [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French") (1903)

In 1813, the college incorporated its medical school with The College of Physicians and Surgeons, a new school created by the Regents of New York, forming [Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons](/wiki/Columbia_University_College_of_Physicians_and_Surgeons "Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons").[[73]](#cite_note-Moore-1846-77): 53–60 In 1857, the college moved from the King's College campus at Park Place to a primarily [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival "Gothic Revival") campus on 49th Street and [Madison Avenue](/wiki/Madison_Avenue "Madison Avenue"), where it remained for the next forty years.

During the last half of the 19th century, under the presidency of [Frederick A. P. Barnard](/wiki/Frederick_A._P._Barnard "Frederick A. P. Barnard"), for whom [Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College") is named, the institution rapidly assumed the shape of a modern university. Barnard College was created in 1889 as a response to the university's refusal to accept women.[[74]](#cite_note-78)

In 1896, university president [Seth Low](/wiki/Seth_Low "Seth Low") moved the campus from 49th Street to its present location, a more spacious campus in the developing neighborhood of [Morningside Heights](/wiki/Morningside_Heights "Morningside Heights").[[66]](#cite_note-Matthews_19042-70)[[75]](#cite_note-hewitt18962-79) Under the leadership of Low's successor, [Nicholas Murray Butler](/wiki/Nicholas_Murray_Butler "Nicholas Murray Butler"), who served for more than four decades, Columbia became the nation's major institution for research, setting the multiversity model that other universities would adopt.[[12]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Columbia2-12)

### 20th century

See also: [Columbia University protests of 1968](/wiki/Columbia_University_protests_of_1968 "Columbia University protests of 1968") and [Student activism at Columbia University](/wiki/Student_activism_at_Columbia_University "Student activism at Columbia University")

On March 8, 1917, as a result of America's entrance into [World War 1](/wiki/World_War_1 "World War 1"), Columbia president Nicholas Murray Butler formed the Columbia Reserve Officers Training Corps.[[76]](#cite_note-80) All students over the age of 18 who passed a physical exam were entered into this program. Within 4 months, over 1,400 students were enrolled within the program. They were one of 12 universities designated by the War department to create a naval section for the Student Army Training Corps. As part of this new program, the US Army charged Columbia to create a "War Issues" course. In 1919, a similar course was created for Columbia students, a "peace issues" course that evolved into "Introduction to Contemporary Civilization in the West", part of the university's [core curriculum for undergraduate students](/wiki/Core_Curriculum_(Columbia_College) "Core Curriculum (Columbia College)").[[77]](#cite_note-81)

In 1919, Columbia established a student application process characterized by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* as "the first modern college application".[[78]](#cite_note-82)

In 1928, [Seth Low Junior College](/wiki/Seth_Low_Junior_College "Seth Low Junior College") was established by Columbia University in order to mitigate the number of Jewish applicants to Columbia College.[[64]](#cite_note-McCaughey20032-68)[[79]](#cite_note-spectatorarchive.library.columbia.edu2-83) The college was closed in 1936 due to the adverse effects of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and its students were subsequently taught at the Morningside Heights campus, although they did not belong to any college but to the university at large.[[80]](#cite_note-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85)

In the 1940s, faculty members, including [John R. Dunning](/wiki/John_R._Dunning "John R. Dunning"), [Isidor Isaac Rabi](/wiki/Isidor_Isaac_Rabi "Isidor Isaac Rabi"), [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi"), and [Polykarp Kusch](/wiki/Polykarp_Kusch "Polykarp Kusch"), began what became the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"), creating the first nuclear fission reactor in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and researching [gaseous diffusion](/wiki/Gaseous_diffusion "Gaseous diffusion").[[82]](#cite_note-Broad2-86)

In the aftermath of World War II, the discipline of international relations became a major scholarly focus of the university, and in response, the [School of International and Public Affairs](/wiki/Columbia_University_School_of_International_and_Public_Affairs "Columbia University School of International and Public Affairs") was founded in 1946, drawing upon the resources of the faculties of political science, economics, and history.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

In 1947, University Extension, an evening school program open to all for a fee, was reorganized as an undergraduate college and designated the [School of General Studies](/wiki/School_of_General_Studies "School of General Studies") in response to the return of [GIs](/wiki/GI_(military) "GI (military)") after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[84]](#cite_note-88)[[85]](#cite_note-89)

The [Columbia University Bicentennial](/wiki/Columbia_University_Bicentennial "Columbia University Bicentennial") was celebrated in 1954.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

During the 1960s, student activism reached a climax with [protests in the spring of 1968](/wiki/Columbia_University_protests_of_1968 "Columbia University protests of 1968"), when hundreds of students occupied buildings on campus. The incident forced the resignation of Columbia's president, [Grayson Kirk](/wiki/Grayson_Kirk "Grayson Kirk"), and the establishment of the University Senate.[[87]](#cite_note-91)[[88]](#cite_note-92)

Though several schools in the university had admitted women for years, Columbia College first admitted women in the fall of 1983,[[89]](#cite_note-93) after a decade of failed negotiations with [Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College"), the all-female institution affiliated with the university, to merge the two schools.[[90]](#cite_note-94) Barnard College still remains affiliated with Columbia, and all Barnard graduates are issued diplomas signed by the [presidents of Columbia University](/wiki/President_of_Columbia_University "President of Columbia University") and Barnard College.[[91]](#cite_note-95)[[92]](#cite_note-96)

During the late 20th century, the university underwent significant academic, structural, and administrative changes as it developed into a major research university. For much of the 19th century, the university consisted of decentralized and separate faculties specializing in Political Science, Philosophy, and Pure Science. In 1979, these faculties were merged into the [Graduate School of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Columbia_Graduate_School_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Columbia Graduate School of Arts and Sciences").[[93]](#cite_note-97) In 1991, the faculties of Columbia College, the School of General Studies, the Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, the [School of the Arts](/wiki/Columbia_University_School_of_the_Arts "Columbia University School of the Arts"), and the [School of Professional Studies](/wiki/Columbia_University_School_of_Professional_Studies "Columbia University School of Professional Studies") were merged into the Faculty of Arts and Sciences, leading to the academic integration and centralized governance of these schools.

In 1995, the Division of Special Programs, later called the School of Continuing Education and now the [School of Professional Studies](/wiki/Columbia_University_School_of_Professional_Studies "Columbia University School of Professional Studies"), was established to reprise the former role of University Extension.[[94]](#cite_note-98)

### 21st century

#### Bollinger presidency (2002-2023)

[Lee C. Bollinger](/wiki/Lee_Bollinger "Lee Bollinger") became Columbia's 19th president in June 2002,[[95]](#cite_note-99) succeeding George Rupp.[[96]](#cite_note-100) Bollinger was appointed in October 2001 after arriving from the presidency of the University of Michigan.[[97]](#cite_note-101) His presidency emphasized campus expansion, defense of affirmative action in college admissions, support for government funding of private sector media, and opposition to disinformation.[[98]](#cite_note-102)

Under Bollinger's tenure, the university publicly launched a record $4 billion capital campaign in September 2006. Financial aid was improved, eliminating loans for undergraduates from families earning under $50,000, and additional funds were allocated to support Bollinger's efforts toward the Manhattanville campus expansion, which nearly doubled Columbia's physical footprint.[[99]](#cite_note-103)[[100]](#cite_note-104) This expansion was supported by a major gift from trustee [Gerry Lenfest](/wiki/Gerry_Lenfest "Gerry Lenfest").[[101]](#cite_note-105)

Globalization efforts continued with the World Leaders Forum and the creation of the Committee on Global Thought, chaired by [Joseph Stiglitz](/wiki/Joseph_Stiglitz "Joseph Stiglitz").

The [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") impacted Columbia's endowment.[[102]](#cite_note-106) The endowment recovered, hitting $8.2B in Oct 2013. Despite the downturn, the university pressed on with Manhattanville construction, receiving final state approval in June 2009.[[103]](#cite_note-107)[[104]](#cite_note-108)

In May 2011, following the repeal of "Don't Ask, Don't Tell," the University Senate voted 51–17 to invite [ROTC](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") back after a 40-year absence, and Bollinger announced an agreement with the Navy.[[105]](#cite_note-109) Columbia has maintained its [Global Centers](/wiki/Columbia_Global_Centers "Columbia Global Centers") network (Amman, Athens, Beijing, Mumbai, Paris, Nairobi, Istanbul, Santiago, Rio de Janeiro, Tunis, Tel Aviv), aiming to increase global engagement and international student enrollment.[[106]](#cite_note-110)[[107]](#cite_note-111)

From 2014 to 2021, Columbia University pursued significant physical expansion, notably opening major facilities on the Manhattanville campus (ZMBBI, Lenfest Center, The Forum). Key strategic initiatives launched included the [Knight First Amendment Institute](/wiki/Knight_First_Amendment_Institute_v._Trump "Knight First Amendment Institute v. Trump"), Columbia World Projects, and the new [Columbia Climate School](/wiki/Columbia_Climate_School "Columbia Climate School") (2020). A $5 billion university capital campaign was launched (with a $1.5B A&S target), major gifts like $50M for A&S's Uris Hall renovation were secured, and the endowment grew significantly ($14.35B by mid-2021).[[108]](#cite_note-112)

In February 2022, Columbia's mathematics professor [Michael Thaddeus](/wiki/Michael_Thaddeus "Michael Thaddeus") published a website and argued that data submitted by Columbia for U.S. News college rankings was "inaccurate, dubious or highly misleading".[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-114)[[111]](#cite_note-115) In July 2022, *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* unranked Columbia citing failed verification.[[112]](#cite_note-116) In September 2022, Columbia acknowledged the submission of incorrect data to U.S. News college rankings.[[113]](#cite_note-117) In June 2023, Columbia announced its non-participation in the rankings.[[114]](#cite_note-118)[[115]](#cite_note-119) In July 2025, Columbia offered to pay US$9 million to settle a class-action lawsuit by its students in [Manhattan federal court](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Southern_District_of_New_York "United States District Court for the Southern District of New York") over the alleged submission of false data to boost its U.S. News college rankings.[[116]](#cite_note-120)[[117]](#cite_note-121)

#### Shafik presidency (2023-2024)

In January 2023, Columbia University announced that Minouche Shafik would be the school's 20th president. Previously, Shafik served as president of the London School of Economics and Political Science. Shafik was inaugurated in October 2023.[[118]](#cite_note-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/%22Liberated_Zone%2C%22_Columbia_University%2C_April_17.jpg/250px-%22Liberated_Zone%2C%22_Columbia_University%2C_April_17.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Liberated_Zone,%22_Columbia_University,_April_17.jpg)

A tent in the [Gaza Solidarity Encampment](/wiki/Gaza_Solidarity_Encampment_(Columbia_University) "Gaza Solidarity Encampment (Columbia University)") (April 17-30, 2024), part of a [broader protest movement](/wiki/Columbia_University_pro-Palestinian_campus_protests_during_the_Gaza_war "Columbia University pro-Palestinian campus protests during the Gaza war") at the university during the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and [genocide](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide"), April 17, 2024.[[119]](#cite_note-123)[[120]](#cite_note-124)

Beginning in fall 2023, [protests at Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University_pro-Palestinian_campus_protests_and_occupations_during_the_Gaza_war "Columbia University pro-Palestinian campus protests and occupations during the Gaza war") against the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and what student protesters described as Columbia's complicity in the [Gaza genocide](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide"),[[121]](#cite_note-125) resulted in police action, student disciplinary measures, and the appointment of [Katrina Armstrong](/wiki/Katrina_Armstrong "Katrina Armstrong") as interim president in August 2024 after the resignation of former university president [Minouche Shafik](/wiki/Minouche_Shafik "Minouche Shafik").[[122]](#cite_note-actingpres-126) The federal government also launched an [investigation into antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism_at_Columbia_University#During_the_Gaza_war "Antisemitism at Columbia University") and then withdrew funding, prompting the university to adopt new policies and adjust disciplinary processes.[[123]](#cite_note-NBCColumbia-127) Claire Shipman was appointed acting president in March 2025.[[124]](#cite_note-128) Columbia also closed its Morningside Heights campus to the general public, allowing access only to holders of Columbia ID cards and approved guests.[[125]](#cite_note-129) In July 2025, Columbia finalized a [$220 million settlement](/wiki/Columbia_University%27s_settlement_with_the_Trump_administration "Columbia University's settlement with the Trump administration") to resume federal funding, agreeing to implement a set of reforms to address anti-semitism. The university also adopted the [IHRA definition](/wiki/IHRA_definition_of_antisemitism "IHRA definition of antisemitism") of antisemitism.[[126]](#cite_note-130)[[127]](#cite_note-131)

In January 2026, the university announced that it had chosen [Jennifer Mnookin](/wiki/Jennifer_Mnookin "Jennifer Mnookin"), the chancellor of the [University of Wisconsin-Madison](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin-Madison "University of Wisconsin-Madison"), as the next president of Columbia, effective July 1, 2026.[[128]](#cite_note-132)[[129]](#cite_note-133)

In March 2026, a $481 million ruling that Columbia University had won against software company Gen Digital for infringing patents related to cybersecurity technology was overturned. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit stated that Columbia's patents may be invalid and sent the case back to Virginia federal court.[[130]](#cite_note-134)

## Campus

### Morningside Heights

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Columbia_College_Walk.jpg/250px-Columbia_College_Walk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Columbia_College_Walk.jpg)

College Walk

The majority of Columbia's graduate and undergraduate studies are conducted in the [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan") neighborhood of [Morningside Heights](/wiki/Morningside_Heights,_Manhattan "Morningside Heights, Manhattan") on [Seth Low](/wiki/Seth_Low "Seth Low")'s late-19th century vision of a university campus where all disciplines could be taught at one location. The campus was designed along [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") planning principles by the architects [McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White"). Columbia's main campus occupies more than six [city blocks](/wiki/City_block "City block"), or 32 acres (13 ha), in Morningside Heights, New York City, a neighborhood that contains a number of academic institutions. The university owns over 7,800 apartments in Morningside Heights, housing faculty, graduate students, and staff. Almost two dozen undergraduate dormitories (purpose-built or converted) are located on campus or in Morningside Heights. Columbia University has [an extensive tunnel system](/wiki/Columbia_University_tunnels "Columbia University tunnels"), more than a century old, with the oldest portions predating the present campus. Some of these remain accessible to the public, while others have been cordoned off.[[131]](#cite_note-135)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg/250px-Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg)

[Butler Library](/wiki/Butler_Library "Butler Library")

[Butler Library](/wiki/Butler_Library "Butler Library") is the largest in the [Columbia University Libraries](/wiki/Columbia_University_Libraries "Columbia University Libraries") system and one of the largest buildings on the campus. It was completed in 1934 and renamed to Butler Library in 1946.[[132]](#cite_note-136) As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Columbia_University&action=edit), Columbia's library system includes over 15.0 million volumes, making it the eighth largest library system and fifth largest collegiate library system in the United States.[[133]](#cite_note-Mian-2020-137)

Several buildings on the Morningside Heights campus are listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). [Low Memorial Library](/wiki/Low_Memorial_Library "Low Memorial Library"), a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") and the centerpiece of the campus, is listed for its architectural significance. [Philosophy Hall](/wiki/Philosophy_Hall "Philosophy Hall") is listed as the site of the invention of [FM radio](/wiki/FM_radio "FM radio").[[134]](#cite_note-138) Also listed is [Pupin Hall](/wiki/Pupin_Hall "Pupin Hall"), another [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), which houses the physics and astronomy departments. Here the first experiments on the fission of uranium were conducted by [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi"). The uranium atom was split there ten days after the world's first atom-splitting in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), Denmark.[[135]](#cite_note-139)[[136]](#cite_note-140) Other buildings listed include [Casa Italiana](/wiki/Casa_Italiana "Casa Italiana"), the [Delta Psi, Alpha Chapter building](/wiki/Delta_Psi,_Alpha_Chapter_building "Delta Psi, Alpha Chapter building") of [St. Anthony Hall](/wiki/St._Anthony_Hall "St. Anthony Hall"), [Earl Hall](/wiki/Earl_Hall "Earl Hall"), and the buildings of the affiliated [Union Theological Seminary](/wiki/Union_Theological_Seminary_(New_York_City) "Union Theological Seminary (New York City)").[[137]](#cite_note-nycland-141)[[138]](#cite_note-142)[[139]](#cite_note-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Union_Theological_Seminary_NYC_001_002_combined.jpg/250px-Union_Theological_Seminary_NYC_001_002_combined.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Theological_Seminary_NYC_001_002_combined.jpg)

[Union Theological Seminary](/wiki/Union_Theological_Seminary_in_the_City_of_New_York "Union Theological Seminary in the City of New York")

A statue by sculptor [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French") called *[Alma Mater](/wiki/Alma_Mater_(New_York_sculpture) "Alma Mater (New York sculpture)")* is centered on the front steps of [Low Memorial Library](/wiki/Low_Memorial_Library "Low Memorial Library"). The statue represents a personification of the traditional image of the university as an *[alma mater](/wiki/Alma_mater "Alma mater")*, or "nourishing mother", draped in an academic gown and seated on a throne. She wears a [laurel wreath](/wiki/Laurel_wreath "Laurel wreath") on her head and holds in her right hand a scepter capped by a King's Crown, a traditional symbol of the university. A book, representing learning, rests on her lap. The arms of her throne end in lamps, representing "Sapientia et Doctrina", or "Wisdom and Learning"; on the back of the throne is embossed an image of [the seal of the university](/wiki/Heraldry_of_Columbia_University "Heraldry of Columbia University").[[141]](#cite_note-Durante-2007-145)[[142]](#cite_note-146) The small hidden owl on the sculpture is also the subject of many Columbia legends, the main legend being that the first student in the freshmen class to find the hidden owl on the statue will be valedictorian, and that any subsequent Columbia male who finds it will marry a Barnard student, given that Barnard is a [women's college](/wiki/Women%27s_college "Women's college").[[143]](#cite_note-147)[[144]](#cite_note-148)

"The Steps", alternatively known as "Low Steps" or the "Urban Beach", are a popular meeting area for Columbia students. The term refers to the long series of granite steps leading from the lower part of campus (South Field) to its upper terrace.[[145]](#cite_note-149)

### Other campuses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/LDEO_Entrance.jpg/250px-LDEO_Entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:LDEO_Entrance.jpg)

Lamont Campus entrance in [Palisades](/wiki/Palisades,_New_York "Palisades, New York"), New York

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Columbia_University_College_of_Physicians_and_Surgeons_entrance.jpg/250px-Columbia_University_College_of_Physicians_and_Surgeons_entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Columbia_University_College_of_Physicians_and_Surgeons_entrance.jpg)

The entrance to the [College of Physicians and Surgeons](/wiki/Columbia_University_Vagelos_College_of_Physicians_and_Surgeons "Columbia University Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons") in [Washington Heights](/wiki/Washington_Heights,_Manhattan "Washington Heights, Manhattan")

In April 2007, the university purchased more than two-thirds of a 17 acres (6.9 ha) site for a new campus in [Manhattanville](/wiki/Manhattanville "Manhattanville"), an industrial neighborhood to the north of the Morningside Heights campus. Stretching from [125th Street](/wiki/125th_Street_(Manhattan) "125th Street (Manhattan)") to [133rd Street](/wiki/133rd_Street_(Manhattan) "133rd Street (Manhattan)"), Columbia Manhattanville houses buildings for Columbia's Business School, School of International and Public Affairs, Columbia School of the Arts, and the Jerome L. Greene Center for Mind, Brain, and Behavior, where research will occur on neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's.[[146]](#cite_note-150)[[147]](#cite_note-151) The $7 billion expansion plan included demolishing all buildings, except three that are historically significant (the [Studebaker Building](/wiki/Studebaker_Building_(Columbia_University) "Studebaker Building (Columbia University)"), [Prentis Hall](/wiki/Prentis_Hall "Prentis Hall"), and the Nash Building), eliminating the existing light industry and storage warehouses, and relocating tenants in 132 apartments. Replacing these buildings created 6.8 million square feet (630,000 m2) of space for the university. Community activist groups in West Harlem fought the expansion for reasons ranging from property protection and fair exchange for land, to residents' rights.[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-153) Subsequent public hearings drew neighborhood opposition. As of December 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Columbia_University&action=edit), the State of New York's [Empire State Development Corporation](/wiki/Empire_State_Development_Corporation "Empire State Development Corporation") approved use of eminent domain, which, through declaration of Manhattanville's "blighted" status, gives governmental bodies the right to appropriate private property for public use.[[150]](#cite_note-154) On May 20, 2009, the [New York State Public Authorities Control Board](/wiki/New_York_State_Public_Authorities_Control_Board "New York State Public Authorities Control Board") approved the Manhanttanville expansion plan.[[151]](#cite_note-155)

[NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital](/wiki/NewYork-Presbyterian_Hospital "NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital") is affiliated with the medical schools of both Columbia University and [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"). According to *U.S. News & World Report*'s "2020–21 Best Hospitals Honor Roll and Medical Specialties Rankings", it is ranked fourth overall and second among university hospitals.[[152]](#cite_note-156) Columbia's [medical school](/wiki/Columbia_University_Vagelos_College_of_Physicians_and_Surgeons "Columbia University Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons") has a strategic partnership with [New York State Psychiatric Institute](/wiki/New_York_State_Psychiatric_Institute "New York State Psychiatric Institute"), and is affiliated with 19 other hospitals in the U.S. and four hospitals in other countries. Health-related schools are located at the [Columbia University Medical Center](/wiki/Columbia_University_Medical_Center "Columbia University Medical Center"), a 20-acre (8.1 ha) campus located in the neighborhood of [Washington Heights](/wiki/Washington_Heights,_Manhattan "Washington Heights, Manhattan"), fifty blocks uptown. Other teaching hospitals affiliated with Columbia through the NewYork-Presbyterian network include the Payne Whitney Clinic in Manhattan, and the Payne Whitney Westchester, a psychiatric institute located in White Plains, New York.[[153]](#cite_note-157) On the northern tip of Manhattan island (in the neighborhood of [Inwood](/wiki/Inwood,_Manhattan "Inwood, Manhattan")), Columbia owns the 26-acre (11 ha) Baker Field, which includes the [Lawrence A. Wien Stadium](/wiki/Lawrence_A._Wien_Stadium "Lawrence A. Wien Stadium") as well as facilities for field sports, outdoor track, and tennis. There is a third campus on the west bank of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), the 157-acre (64 ha) [Lamont–Doherty Earth Observatory](/wiki/Lamont%E2%80%93Doherty_Earth_Observatory "Lamont–Doherty Earth Observatory") and Earth Institute in [Palisades](/wiki/Palisades,_New_York "Palisades, New York"), New York. A fourth is the 60-acre (24 ha) [Nevis Laboratories](/wiki/Nevis_Laboratories "Nevis Laboratories") in [Irvington, New York](/wiki/Irvington,_New_York "Irvington, New York"), for the study of particle and motion physics. A satellite site in Paris holds classes at [Reid Hall](/wiki/Reid_Hall "Reid Hall").[[12]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Columbia2-12)

### Sustainability

In 2006, the university established the Office of Environmental Stewardship to initiate, coordinate and implement programs to reduce the university's environmental footprint. The U.S. Green Building Council selected the university's Manhattanville plan for the [Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") (LEED) Neighborhood Design pilot program.[[154]](#cite_note-158)[[155]](#cite_note-159)

[[156]](#cite_note-160) Columbia has been rated "B+" by the 2011 College Sustainability Report Card for its environmental and sustainability initiatives.[[157]](#cite_note-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/116th_Street_Columbia_University_Station.JPG/250px-116th_Street_Columbia_University_Station.JPG)](/wiki/File:116th_Street_Columbia_University_Station.JPG)

Access to Columbia is enhanced by the [116th Street–Columbia University](/wiki/116th_Street%E2%80%93Columbia_University_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "116th Street–Columbia University (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") subway station ([1](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)") train) on the [IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line "IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line").

According to the [A. W. Kuchler](/wiki/A._W._Kuchler "A. W. Kuchler") U.S. [potential natural vegetation](/wiki/Potential_natural_vegetation "Potential natural vegetation") types, Columbia University would have a dominant vegetation type of Appalachian [Oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") (*104*) with a dominant vegetation form of Eastern [Hardwood](/wiki/Hardwood "Hardwood") Forest (*25*).[[158]](#cite_note-Conservation_Biology_Institute2-162)

### Transportation

[Columbia Transportation](/wiki/Columbia_Transportation "Columbia Transportation") is the bus service of the university, operated by [Academy Bus Lines](/wiki/Academy_Bus_Lines "Academy Bus Lines"). The buses are open to all Columbia faculty, students, Dodge Fitness Center members, and anyone else who holds a Columbia ID card. In addition, all [TSC](/wiki/The_School_at_Columbia "The School at Columbia") students can ride the buses.[[159]](#cite_note-163)

In the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"), the [!["1" train](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/NYCS-bull-trans-1-Std.svg/20px-NYCS-bull-trans-1-Std.svg.png)](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)") train serves the university at [116th Street-Columbia University](/wiki/116th_Street%E2%80%93Columbia_University_station "116th Street–Columbia University station"). The [M4](/wiki/M4_(New_York_City_bus) "M4 (New York City bus)"), [M104](/wiki/M104_(New_York_City_bus) "M104 (New York City bus)") and [M60](/wiki/M60_(New_York_City_bus) "M60 (New York City bus)") buses stop on Broadway while the [M11](/wiki/M11_(New_York_City_bus) "M11 (New York City bus)") stops on Amsterdam Avenue.

The main campus is primarily boxed off by the streets of Amsterdam Avenue, [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)"), 114th street, and 120th street, with some buildings, including Barnard College, located just outside the area. The nearest major highway is the [Henry Hudson Parkway](/wiki/Henry_Hudson_Parkway "Henry Hudson Parkway") ([NY 9A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_9A "New York State Route 9A")) to the west of the campus. It is located 3.4 miles (5.5 km) south of the [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge").

Columbia is a racially diverse school, with approximately 52% of all students identifying themselves as persons of color. Additionally, around 50% of all undergraduates received grants from Columbia.[[162]](#cite_note-stat2-166) The college is [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") for domestic applicants.[[163]](#cite_note-167)

On April 11, 2007, Columbia University announced a $400 million donation from media billionaire alumnus [John Kluge](/wiki/John_Kluge "John Kluge") to be used exclusively for undergraduate financial aid. The donation is among the largest single gifts to higher education.[[164]](#cite_note-168) However, this did not apply to international students, transfer students, visiting students, or students in the School of General Studies.[[165]](#cite_note-169) In the fall of 2010, admission to Columbia's undergraduate colleges [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College,_Columbia_University "Columbia College, Columbia University") and the [Fu Foundation School of Engineering and Applied Science](/wiki/Fu_Foundation_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "Fu Foundation School of Engineering and Applied Science") (also known as SEAS or Columbia Engineering) began accepting the [Common Application](/wiki/Common_Application "Common Application"). The policy change made Columbia one of the last major academic institutions and the last [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") university to switch to the Common Application.[[166]](#cite_note-170)

Columbia University is an independent, privately supported, nonsectarian and [not-for-profit](/wiki/Not-for-profit "Not-for-profit") institution of higher education.[[168]](#cite_note-172) Its official corporate name is [Trustees of Columbia University in the City of New York](/wiki/Trustees_of_Columbia_University_in_the_City_of_New_York "Trustees of Columbia University in the City of New York").[[169]](#cite_note-173)

Joint degree programs are available through [Union Theological Seminary](/wiki/Union_Theological_Seminary_in_the_City_of_New_York "Union Theological Seminary in the City of New York"), the [Jewish Theological Seminary of America](/wiki/Jewish_Theological_Seminary_of_America "Jewish Theological Seminary of America"),[[170]](#cite_note-174) and the [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School").[[171]](#cite_note-175)[[172]](#cite_note-176) [Teachers College](/wiki/Teachers_College "Teachers College") and [Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College") are official faculties of the university; both colleges' presidents are deans under the university governance structure.[[173]](#cite_note-columbia.edu2-177) The Columbia University Senate includes faculty and student representatives from Teachers College and Barnard College who serve two-year terms; all senators are accorded full voting privileges regarding matters impacting the entire university. Teachers College is an affiliated, financially independent graduate school with their own board of trustees.[[174]](#cite_note-senate.columbia.edu2-178)[[175]](#cite_note-ReferenceB2-179) Pursuant to an affiliation agreement, Columbia is given the authority to confer "degrees and diplomas" to the graduates of Teachers College. The degrees are signed by presidents of Teachers College and Columbia University in a manner analogous to the university's other graduate schools.[[176]](#cite_note-secretary.columbia.edu2-180)[[177]](#cite_note-181)[[173]](#cite_note-columbia.edu2-177) Columbia's General Studies school also has joint undergraduate programs available through [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London"),[[178]](#cite_note-182) [Sciences Po](/wiki/Sciences_Po "Sciences Po"),[[179]](#cite_note-183) [City University of Hong Kong](/wiki/City_University_of_Hong_Kong "City University of Hong Kong"),[[180]](#cite_note-184) [Trinity College Dublin](/wiki/Trinity_College_Dublin "Trinity College Dublin"),[[181]](#cite_note-185) and the [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School").[[182]](#cite_note-college.columbia2-186)

The university also has several [Columbia Global Centers](/wiki/Columbia_Global_Centers "Columbia Global Centers"), in [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman"), [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi"), Paris, [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago"), and [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis").[[183]](#cite_note-187)

### International partnerships

Columbia students can study abroad for a semester or a year at partner institutions such as [Sciences Po](/wiki/Sciences_Po "Sciences Po"),[[184]](#cite_note-188) [École des hautes études en sciences sociales](/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_hautes_%C3%A9tudes_en_sciences_sociales "École des hautes études en sciences sociales") (EHESS), [École normale supérieure](/wiki/%C3%89cole_normale_sup%C3%A9rieure "École normale supérieure") (ENS), [Panthéon-Sorbonne University](/wiki/University_of_Paris_1_Pantheon-Sorbonne "University of Paris 1 Pantheon-Sorbonne"), [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London"), [London School of Economics](/wiki/London_School_of_Economics "London School of Economics"), [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") and the [University of Warwick](/wiki/University_of_Warwick "University of Warwick"). Select students can study at either the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") or the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") for a year if approved by both Columbia and either Oxford or Cambridge.[[185]](#cite_note-189) Columbia also has a dual MA program with the [Aga Khan University](/wiki/Aga_Khan_University "Aga Khan University") in London.

Columbia University was ranked 12th in the United States and tenth globally for 2023–2024 by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

In 2021, Columbia was ranked seventh in the world (sixth in the United States) by *[Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")*, sixth in the world by *U.S. News & World Report*, 19th in the world by *[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")*, and 11th globally by *Times Higher Education World University Rankings*. It was ranked in the first tier of American research universities, along with Harvard, MIT, and Stanford, in the 2019 report from the [Center for Measuring University Performance](/wiki/Center_for_Measuring_University_Performance "Center for Measuring University Performance").

In 2025, Columbia was ranked 250 out of 257 top colleges in "Free Speech Rankings" by the [Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression](/wiki/Foundation_for_Individual_Rights_and_Expression "Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression") and "College Pulse", after ranking 214 of 248 in 2024 and at the bottom of 203 in 2022/2023.[[197]](#cite_note-201)[[198]](#cite_note-202)[[199]](#cite_note-203) In 2024 and 2025, Columbia received a D on the "Campus Antisemitism Report Card" of the [Anti-Defamation League](/wiki/Anti-Defamation_League "Anti-Defamation League"), which the advocacy organization first launched in spring 2024, in the lead-up to and in the context of campus conflict over the [2024 Columbia University pro-Palestinian campus occupations](/wiki/2024_Columbia_University_pro-Palestinian_campus_occupations "2024 Columbia University pro-Palestinian campus occupations").[[200]](#cite_note-204)[[201]](#cite_note-205)[[202]](#cite_note-206)[[203]](#cite_note-207)[[204]](#cite_note-208)

### Research

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/HavemeyerFront.jpg/250px-HavemeyerFront.jpg)](/wiki/File:HavemeyerFront.jpg)

[Havemeyer Hall](/wiki/Havemeyer_Hall "Havemeyer Hall"), a [National Historic Chemical Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Chemical_Landmarks "National Historic Chemical Landmarks"), where [deuterium](/wiki/Deuterium "Deuterium") was discovered in 1931. Research conducted in Havemeyer has been recognized with seven [Nobel Prizes in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry").[[205]](#cite_note-209)

Columbia University has been the site of many scientific milestones, including the first nuclear fission in North America, the invention of FM radio and the laser,[[206]](#cite_note-210) and key contributions to neuroscience.[[207]](#cite_note-211) Researchers at Columbia have also advanced efforts in technology, such as digital media,[[208]](#cite_note-:0-212)[[209]](#cite_note-inventions_nind2-213) cellular imaging,[[208]](#cite_note-:0-212) and brain-computer interfaces.[[210]](#cite_note-214)[[211]](#cite_note-215)[[212]](#cite_note-216)

Columbia is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity".[[213]](#cite_note-217) Columbia scientists have been credited with about 175 new inventions in the health sciences each year.[[209]](#cite_note-inventions_nind2-213) Columbia is affiliated with many research facilities, such as the [Columbia Institute for Tele-Information](/wiki/Columbia_Institute_for_Tele-Information "Columbia Institute for Tele-Information") dedicated to [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications") and the [Goddard Institute for Space Studies](/wiki/Goddard_Institute_for_Space_Studies "Goddard Institute for Space Studies"), which is an [astronomical](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") [observatory](/wiki/Observatory "Observatory") affiliated with [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA").[[214]](#cite_note-218)

### Military and veteran enrollment

Columbia is a long-standing participant of the [United States Department of Veterans Affairs](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Veterans_Affairs "United States Department of Veterans Affairs") Yellow Ribbon Program, allowing eligible veterans and their dependents to pursue a Columbia undergraduate degree regardless of socioeconomic status for over 70 years.[[215]](#cite_note-219) As a part of the Eisenhower Leader Development Program (ELDP) in partnership with the [United States Military Academy](/wiki/United_States_Military_Academy "United States Military Academy") at [West Point](/wiki/West_Point,_New_York "West Point, New York"), Columbia is the only school in the Ivy League to offer a graduate degree program in organizational psychology to aid military officers in tactical decision making and strategic management.[[216]](#cite_note-220)[[217]](#cite_note-221)

### Awards

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Lee_C._Bollinger_awarding_the_2003_Pulitzer_Prize_to_Jeffrey_Eugenides_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lee_C._Bollinger_awarding_the_2003_Pulitzer_Prize_to_Jeffrey_Eugenides_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lee_C._Bollinger_awarding_the_2003_Pulitzer_Prize_to_Jeffrey_Eugenides_(cropped).jpg)

[President](/wiki/President_of_Columbia_University "President of Columbia University") [Lee Bollinger](/wiki/Lee_Bollinger "Lee Bollinger") presents the 2003 [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction") to [Jeffrey Eugenides](/wiki/Jeffrey_Eugenides "Jeffrey Eugenides")

Several prestigious awards are administered by Columbia University, most notably the Pulitzer Prize and the [Bancroft Prize](/wiki/Bancroft_Prize "Bancroft Prize") in history.[[218]](#cite_note-history-222)[[219]](#cite_note-223) Other prizes, which are awarded by the [Graduate School of Journalism](/wiki/Columbia_University_Graduate_School_of_Journalism "Columbia University Graduate School of Journalism"), include the [Alfred I. duPont–Columbia University Award](/wiki/Alfred_I._duPont%E2%80%93Columbia_University_Award "Alfred I. duPont–Columbia University Award"), the [National Magazine Awards](/wiki/National_Magazine_Awards "National Magazine Awards"), the [Maria Moors Cabot Prizes](/wiki/Maria_Moors_Cabot_Prizes "Maria Moors Cabot Prizes"), the [John Chancellor Award](/wiki/John_Chancellor_Award "John Chancellor Award"), and the Lukas Prizes, which include the [J. Anthony Lukas Book Prize](/wiki/J._Anthony_Lukas_Book_Prize "J. Anthony Lukas Book Prize") and [Mark Lynton History Prize](/wiki/Mark_Lynton_History_Prize "Mark Lynton History Prize").[[220]](#cite_note-224) The university also administers the [Louisa Gross Horwitz Prize](/wiki/Louisa_Gross_Horwitz_Prize "Louisa Gross Horwitz Prize"), which is considered an important precursor to the Nobel Prize, 55 of its 117 recipients having gone on to win either a [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") or [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") as of October 2024;[[221]](#cite_note-225) the [W. Alden Spencer Award](/wiki/W._Alden_Spencer_Award "W. Alden Spencer Award");[[222]](#cite_note-226) the [Vetlesen Prize](/wiki/Vetlesen_Prize "Vetlesen Prize"), which is known as the Nobel Prize of geology;[[223]](#cite_note-227) the [Japan-U.S. Friendship Commission Prize for the Translation of Japanese Literature](/wiki/Japan-U.S._Friendship_Commission_Prize_for_the_Translation_of_Japanese_Literature "Japan-U.S. Friendship Commission Prize for the Translation of Japanese Literature"), the oldest such award;[[224]](#cite_note-228) the [Edwin Howard Armstrong award](/wiki/Edwin_Howard_Armstrong_award "Edwin Howard Armstrong award");[[225]](#cite_note-229) the [Calderone Prize](/wiki/Calderone_Prize "Calderone Prize") in public health;[[226]](#cite_note-230) and the [Ditson Conductor's Award](/wiki/Ditson_Conductor%27s_Award "Ditson Conductor's Award").[[227]](#cite_note-231)
