In 2020, Columbia University's student population was 31,455 (8,842 students in undergraduate programs and 22,613 in postgraduate programs), with 45% of the student population identifying themselves as a minority.[[229]](#cite_note-Columbia_U-2021a-236) Twenty-six percent of students at Columbia have family incomes below $60,000. 16% of students at Columbia receive Federal Pell Grants,[[230]](#cite_note-237) which mostly go to students whose family incomes are below $40,000. Seventeen percent of students are the first member of their family to attend a four-year college.[[231]](#cite_note-238)

Columbia University is home to many [fraternities, sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities_in_North_America "Fraternities and sororities in North America"), and co-educational Greek organizations. Approximately 10–15% of undergraduate students are associated with Greek life.[[232]](#cite_note-239) Many Barnard women also join Columbia sororities. There has been a Greek presence on campus since the establishment in 1836 of the Delta chapter of [Alpha Delta Phi](/wiki/Alpha_Delta_Phi "Alpha Delta Phi").[[233]](#cite_note-240)[[234]](#cite_note-241)

### Publications

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Spec1962.jpg/250px-Spec1962.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spec1962.jpg)

Copies of the *[Columbia Daily Spectator](/wiki/Columbia_Daily_Spectator "Columbia Daily Spectator")* being sold during the [1962–1963 New York City newspaper strike](/wiki/1962%E2%80%931963_New_York_City_newspaper_strike "1962–1963 New York City newspaper strike")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/JesterCoverGWBridge.jpg/250px-JesterCoverGWBridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:JesterCoverGWBridge.jpg)

The [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") cover of the November 1931 edition of the [*Jester*](/wiki/Jester_of_Columbia "Jester of Columbia"), celebrating the opening of the [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge")

The *[Columbia Daily Spectator](/wiki/Columbia_Daily_Spectator "Columbia Daily Spectator")* is the nation's second-oldest continuously operating daily student newspaper.[[235]](#cite_note-242) *[The Blue and White](/wiki/The_Blue_and_White "The Blue and White")*[[236]](#cite_note-243) is a monthly literary magazine established in 1890 that discusses campus life and local politics. *[Bwog](/wiki/Bwog "Bwog")*,[[237]](#cite_note-244) originally an offshoot of *The Blue and White* but now fully independent, is an online campus news and entertainment source. *[The Morningside Post](/wiki/The_Morningside_Post "The Morningside Post")* is a student-run multimedia news publication.

Political publications include *[The Current](/wiki/The_Current_(Columbia_University_journal) "The Current (Columbia University journal)")*, a journal of politics, culture and Jewish Affairs;[[238]](#cite_note-245) the *[Columbia Political Review](/wiki/Columbia_Political_Review "Columbia Political Review")*, the multi-partisan political magazine of the Columbia Political Union;[[239]](#cite_note-246) and *AdHoc*, which denotes itself as the "progressive" campus magazine and deals largely with local political issues and arts events.[[240]](#cite_note-247)

*Columbia Magazine* is the alumni magazine of Columbia, serving all 340,000+ of the university's alumni. Arts and literary publications include *The Columbia Review*, the nation's oldest college literary magazine;[[241]](#cite_note-248) *Surgam*, the literary magazine of [The Philolexian Society](/wiki/The_Philolexian_Society "The Philolexian Society");[[242]](#cite_note-249) *Quarto*, Columbia University's official undergraduate literary magazine;[[243]](#cite_note-250) *4x4*, a student-run alternative to *Quarto*;[[244]](#cite_note-251) *Columbia*, a nationally regarded [literary journal](/wiki/Literary_journal "Literary journal"); the *Columbia Journal of Literary Criticism*;[[245]](#cite_note-252) and *The Mobius Strip*, an online arts and literary magazine.[[246]](#cite_note-253) *[Inside New York](/wiki/Inside_New_York "Inside New York")* is an annual guidebook to New York City, written, edited, and published by Columbia undergraduates. Through a distribution agreement with [Columbia University Press](/wiki/Columbia_University_Press "Columbia University Press"), the book is sold at major retailers and independent bookstores.[[247]](#cite_note-254)

Columbia is home to numerous undergraduate academic publications. The *Columbia Undergraduate Science Journal* prints original science research in its two annual publications.[[248]](#cite_note-255) The *[Journal of Politics & Society](/wiki/Journal_of_Politics_%26_Society "Journal of Politics & Society")* is a journal of undergraduate research in the social sciences;[[249]](#cite_note-256) *Publius* is an undergraduate journal of politics established in 2008 and published biannually;[[250]](#cite_note-257) the *Columbia East Asia Review* allows undergraduates throughout the world to publish original work on China, Japan, Korea, Tibet, and Vietnam and is supported by the [Weatherhead East Asian Institute](/wiki/Weatherhead_East_Asian_Institute "Weatherhead East Asian Institute");[[251]](#cite_note-258) *[The Birch](/wiki/The_Birch "The Birch")* is an undergraduate journal of Eastern European and Eurasian culture that is the first national student-run journal of its kind;[[252]](#cite_note-259) the *Columbia Economics Review* is the undergraduate economic journal on research and policy supported by the Columbia Economics Department; and the *Columbia Science Review* is a science magazine that prints general interest articles and faculty profiles.[[253]](#cite_note-260)

Humor publications on Columbia's campus include *[The Fed](/wiki/The_Fed_(Columbia_newspaper) "The Fed (Columbia newspaper)")*, a triweekly satire and investigative newspaper, and the *[Jester of Columbia](/wiki/Jester_of_Columbia "Jester of Columbia").[[254]](#cite_note-261)*[[255]](#cite_note-262) Other publications include *The Columbian*, the undergraduate colleges' annually published yearbook;[[256]](#cite_note-263) the *Gadfly*, a biannual journal of popular philosophy produced by undergraduates;[[257]](#cite_note-264) and *Rhapsody in Blue*, an undergraduate urban studies magazine.[[258]](#cite_note-265) Professional journals published by academic departments at Columbia University include *Current Musicology* and *[The Journal of Philosophy](/wiki/The_Journal_of_Philosophy "The Journal of Philosophy")*.[[259]](#cite_note-266)[[260]](#cite_note-267) During the spring semester, graduate students in the Journalism School publish *The Bronx Beat*, a bi-weekly newspaper covering the South Bronx.

Founded in 1961 under the auspices of Columbia University's Graduate School of Journalism, the *[Columbia Journalism Review](/wiki/Columbia_Journalism_Review "Columbia Journalism Review")* (CJR) examines day-to-day press performance as well as the forces that affect that performance. The magazine is published six times a year.[[261]](#cite_note-268)

Former publications include the *Columbia University Forum*, a review of literature and cultural affairs distributed for free to alumni.[[262]](#cite_note-269)[[263]](#cite_note-270)

### Broadcasting

Columbia is home to two pioneers in undergraduate [campus radio](/wiki/Campus_radio "Campus radio") broadcasting, [WKCR-FM](/wiki/WKCR-FM "WKCR-FM") and CTV. Many undergraduates are also involved with Barnard's radio station, [WBAR](/wiki/WBAR_(Barnard_College) "WBAR (Barnard College)"). WKCR, the student run radio station that broadcasts to the Tri-state area, claims to be the oldest FM radio station in the world, owing to the university's affiliation with [Edwin Howard Armstrong](/wiki/Edwin_Howard_Armstrong "Edwin Howard Armstrong").[[264]](#cite_note-271) The station has its studios on the second floor of Alfred Lerner Hall on the Morningside campus with its main transmitter tower at [4 Times Square](/wiki/4_Times_Square "4 Times Square") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"). Columbia Television (CTV) is the nation's second oldest [student television station](/wiki/Student_television_station "Student television station") and the home of CTV News, a weekly live news program produced by undergraduate students.[[265]](#cite_note-272)[[266]](#cite_note-273)

### Debate and Model UN

The [Philolexian Society](/wiki/Philolexian_Society "Philolexian Society") is a literary and debating club founded in 1802, making it the oldest student group at Columbia, as well as the third oldest collegiate literary society in the country.[[267]](#cite_note-274) The society annually administers the [Joyce Kilmer Memorial Bad Poetry Contest](/wiki/Joyce_Kilmer_Memorial_Bad_Poetry_Contest "Joyce Kilmer Memorial Bad Poetry Contest").[[268]](#cite_note-275) The Columbia Parliamentary Debate Team competes in tournaments around the country as part of the [American Parliamentary Debate Association](/wiki/American_Parliamentary_Debate_Association "American Parliamentary Debate Association"), and hosts both high school and college tournaments on Columbia's campus, as well as public debates on issues affecting the university.[[269]](#cite_note-276)

The Columbia International Relations Council and Association (CIRCA), oversees Columbia's [Model United Nations](/wiki/Model_United_Nations "Model United Nations") activities. CIRCA hosts college and high school Model UN conferences, hosts speakers influential in international politics to speak on campus, and trains students from underprivileged schools in New York in Model UN.[[270]](#cite_note-277)

### Technology and entrepreneurship

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Columbia_University_-_Department_of_Physics_%2848170362276%29.jpg/250px-Columbia_University_-_Department_of_Physics_%2848170362276%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Columbia_University_-_Department_of_Physics_(48170362276).jpg)

[Pupin Hall](/wiki/Pupin_Hall "Pupin Hall"), the physics building, showing the rooftop [Rutherfurd Observatory](/wiki/Rutherfurd_Observatory "Rutherfurd Observatory")

Many engineering entrepreneurs in New York City have attended Columbia. Since 1990, graduates of Columbia have established more than 100 technology companies.[[271]](#cite_note-278)

The student-run group Columbia University Organization of Rising Entrepreneurs (CORE) was founded in 1999 with an aim to foster entrepreneurship on campus. Each year CORE hosts events, including #StartupColumbia, a conference and venture competition for $250,000, and Ignite@CU, a weekend for undergrads interested in design, engineering, and entrepreneurship. Notable speakers include [Peter Thiel](/wiki/Peter_Thiel "Peter Thiel"), [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"),[[272]](#cite_note-279) [Alexis Ohanian](/wiki/Alexis_Ohanian "Alexis Ohanian"), [Drew Houston](/wiki/Drew_Houston "Drew Houston"), and [Mark Cuban](/wiki/Mark_Cuban "Mark Cuban").

CampusNetwork, an on-campus social networking site called Campus Network that preceded Facebook, was created and popularized by Columbia engineering student Adam Goldberg in 2003. [Mark Zuckerberg](/wiki/Mark_Zuckerberg "Mark Zuckerberg") later asked Goldberg to join him in [Palo Alto](/wiki/Palo_Alto "Palo Alto") to work on Facebook, but Goldberg declined the offer.[[273]](#cite_note-280) The [Fu Foundation School of Engineering and Applied Science](/wiki/Fu_Foundation_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "Fu Foundation School of Engineering and Applied Science") offers a minor in Technical Entrepreneurship through its Center for Technology, Innovation, and Community Engagement. SEAS' entrepreneurship activities focus on community building initiatives in New York and worldwide, made possible through partners such as [Microsoft Corporation](/wiki/Microsoft_Corporation "Microsoft Corporation").[[274]](#cite_note-281)

On June 14, 2010, Mayor [Michael R. Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_R._Bloomberg "Michael R. Bloomberg") launched the NYC Media Lab to promote innovations in New York's media industry. Situated at the [New York University Tandon School of Engineering](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering"), the lab is a consortium of Columbia University, [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), and [New York City Economic Development Corporation](/wiki/New_York_City_Economic_Development_Corporation "New York City Economic Development Corporation") acting to connect companies with universities in new technology research. The Lab is modeled after similar ones at [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") and [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford "Stanford"), and was established with a $250,000 grant from the New York City Economic Development Corporation.[[275]](#cite_note-282)

### World Leaders Forum

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Uniwersytet_Columbia.jpg/250px-Uniwersytet_Columbia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uniwersytet_Columbia.jpg)

World Leaders Forum at [Low Memorial Library](/wiki/Low_Memorial_Library "Low Memorial Library")

Established in 2003 by university president [Lee C. Bollinger](/wiki/Lee_C._Bollinger "Lee C. Bollinger"), the World Leaders Forum at Columbia University provides the opportunity for students and faculty to listen to world leaders in government, religion, industry, finance, and academia.[[276]](#cite_note-283)

Past forum speakers include former president of the United States [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), former prime minister of India [Atal Bihari Vajpayee](/wiki/Atal_Bihari_Vajpayee "Atal Bihari Vajpayee"), former president of Ghana [John Agyekum Kufuor](/wiki/John_Agyekum_Kufuor "John Agyekum Kufuor"), president of Afghanistan [Hamid Karzai](/wiki/Hamid_Karzai "Hamid Karzai"), prime minister of Russia [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin"), president of the Republic of Mozambique [Joaquim Alberto Chissano](/wiki/Joaquim_Alberto_Chissano "Joaquim Alberto Chissano"), president of the Republic of Bolivia [Carlos Diego Mesa Gisbert](/wiki/Carlos_Diego_Mesa_Gisbert "Carlos Diego Mesa Gisbert"), president of the Republic of Romania [Ion Iliescu](/wiki/Ion_Iliescu "Ion Iliescu"), president of the Republic of Latvia [Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga](/wiki/Vaira_V%C4%AB%C4%B7e-Freiberga "Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga"), the first female president of Finland [Tarja Halonen](/wiki/Tarja_Halonen "Tarja Halonen"), President [Yudhoyono](/wiki/Yudhoyono "Yudhoyono") of Indonesia, President [Pervez Musharraf](/wiki/Pervez_Musharraf "Pervez Musharraf") of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, Iraq President [Jalal Talabani](/wiki/Jalal_Talabani "Jalal Talabani"), the [14th Dalai Lama](/wiki/14th_Dalai_Lama "14th Dalai Lama"), president of the Islamic Republic of Iran [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/wiki/Mahmoud_Ahmadinejad "Mahmoud Ahmadinejad"), financier [George Soros](/wiki/George_Soros "George Soros"), Mayor of New York City [Michael R. Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_R._Bloomberg "Michael R. Bloomberg"), President [Václav Klaus](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Klaus "Václav Klaus") of the Czech Republic, President [Cristina Fernández de Kirchner](/wiki/Cristina_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_Kirchner "Cristina Fernández de Kirchner") of Argentina, former Secretary-General of the United Nations [Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan"), and [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore").[[277]](#cite_note-284)

### Other

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/2014_Columbia_University_Earl_Hall_from_north.jpg/250px-2014_Columbia_University_Earl_Hall_from_north.jpg)](/wiki/File:2014_Columbia_University_Earl_Hall_from_north.jpg)

[Earl Hall](/wiki/Earl_Hall "Earl Hall") was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") for its role in serving as a venue for meetings and dances of the [Columbia Queer Alliance](/wiki/Columbia_Queer_Alliance "Columbia Queer Alliance").

The Columbia University Orchestra was founded by composer [Edward MacDowell](/wiki/Edward_MacDowell "Edward MacDowell") in 1896, and is the oldest continually operating university orchestra in the United States. Undergraduate student composers at Columbia may choose to become involved with Columbia New Music, which sponsors concerts of music written by undergraduate students from all of Columbia's schools.[[278]](#cite_note-285) The Notes and Keys, the oldest [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") group at Columbia, was founded in 1909.[[279]](#cite_note-286) There are a number of performing arts groups at Columbia dedicated to producing student theater, including the Columbia Players, King's Crown Shakespeare Troupe (KCST), Columbia Musical Theater Society (CMTS), NOMADS (New and Original Material Authored and Directed by Students), LateNite Theatre, Columbia University Performing Arts League (CUPAL), Black Theatre Ensemble (BTE), sketch comedy group Chowdah, and improvisational troupes Alfred and Fruit Paunch.[[280]](#cite_note-287)

The [Columbia Queer Alliance](/wiki/Columbia_Queer_Alliance "Columbia Queer Alliance") is the central Columbia student organization that represents the bisexual, lesbian, gay, transgender, and questioning student population. It is the oldest [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay") student organization in the world, founded as the Student [Homophile](/wiki/Homophile_movement "Homophile movement") League in 1967 by students including lifelong activist [Stephen Donaldson](/wiki/Stephen_Donaldson_(activist) "Stephen Donaldson (activist)").[[281]](#cite_note-288)[[282]](#cite_note-289)

Columbia University campus military groups include the U.S. Military Veterans of Columbia University and Advocates for Columbia ROTC. In the 2005–06 academic year, the Columbia Military Society, Columbia's student group for ROTC cadets and Marine officer candidates, was renamed the Hamilton Society for "students who aspire to serve their nation through the military in the tradition of [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton")".[[283]](#cite_note-290)

Columbia has several secret societies, including [St. Anthony Hall](/wiki/St._Anthony_Hall "St. Anthony Hall"), which was founded at the university in 1847, and two senior societies, the [Nacoms and Sachems](/wiki/Nacoms_and_Sachems "Nacoms and Sachems").[[284]](#cite_note-291)[[285]](#cite_note-NYT-1984-292)

## Athletics

Main article: [Columbia Lions](/wiki/Columbia_Lions "Columbia Lions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Bigredmarchingband.jpg/250px-Bigredmarchingband.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bigredmarchingband.jpg)

[Robert K. Kraft Field at Lawrence A. Wien Stadium](/wiki/Robert_K._Kraft_Field_at_Lawrence_A._Wien_Stadium "Robert K. Kraft Field at Lawrence A. Wien Stadium") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), home field of [Columbia Lions football](/wiki/Columbia_Lions_football "Columbia Lions football")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/GehrigCU.jpg/250px-GehrigCU.jpg)](/wiki/File:GehrigCU.jpg)

[Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig"), who attended Columbia University in 1922 and 1923

A member institution of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) in [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [FCS](/wiki/Football_Championship_Subdivision "Football Championship Subdivision"), Columbia fields varsity teams in 29 sports and is a member of the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League").[[286]](#cite_note-293) The football Lions play home games at the 17,000-seat [Robert K. Kraft Field at Lawrence A. Wien Stadium](/wiki/Robert_K._Kraft_Field_at_Lawrence_A._Wien_Stadium "Robert K. Kraft Field at Lawrence A. Wien Stadium"). The Baker Athletics Complex also includes facilities for baseball, softball, soccer, lacrosse, field hockey, tennis, track, and rowing, as well as the new Campbell Sports Center, which opened in January 2013. The basketball, fencing, swimming & diving, volleyball, and wrestling programs are based at the Dodge Physical Fitness Center on the main campus.[[287]](#cite_note-294)

Former students include [Baseball Hall of Famers](/wiki/Baseball_Hall_of_Famers "Baseball Hall of Famers") [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig") and [Eddie Collins](/wiki/Eddie_Collins "Eddie Collins"), [football Hall of Famer](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") [Sid Luckman](/wiki/Sid_Luckman "Sid Luckman"), [Marcellus Wiley](/wiki/Marcellus_Wiley "Marcellus Wiley"), and world champion women's weightlifter [Karyn Marshall](/wiki/Karyn_Marshall "Karyn Marshall").[[288]](#cite_note-tws02oct012-295)[[289]](#cite_note-SI2-296) On May 17, 1939, fledgling [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") broadcast a doubleheader between the Columbia Lions and the [Princeton Tigers](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers "Princeton Tigers") at Columbia's Baker Field, making it the first televised regular athletic event in history.[[290]](#cite_note-297)[[291]](#cite_note-298)

Columbia University participated in multiple firsts within collegiate athletics.[[292]](#cite_note-299) The football program is best known for its record of futility set during the 1980s: between 1983 and 1988, the team lost 44 games in a row, which is still the record for the NCAA [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/Football_Championship_Subdivision "Football Championship Subdivision"). The streak was broken on October 8, 1988, with a 16–13 victory over arch-rival [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"). That was the Lions' first victory at Wien Stadium, which had been opened during the losing streak and was already four years old.[[293]](#cite_note-300) A new tradition has developed with the [Liberty Cup](/wiki/Liberty_Cup "Liberty Cup"). The Liberty Cup is awarded annually to the winner of the football game between [Fordham](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University") and Columbia Universities, two of the only three NCAA Division I football teams in New York City.[[294]](#cite_note-301)

## Traditions

Main article: [Columbia University traditions](/wiki/Columbia_University_traditions "Columbia University traditions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Fly_With_Me_poster.jpg/250px-Fly_With_Me_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fly_With_Me_poster.jpg)

The program for *[Fly With Me](/wiki/Fly_With_Me_(musical) "Fly With Me (musical)")* (1920), one of the only collaborations between [Richard Rodgers](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers "Richard Rodgers"), [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II"), and [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart")

### The Varsity Show

Main article: [Varsity Show](/wiki/Varsity_Show "Varsity Show")

The Varsity Show is one of the oldest traditions at Columbia. Founded in 1893 as a fundraiser for the university's fledgling athletic teams, the Varsity Show now draws together the entire Columbia undergraduate community for a series of performances every April. Dedicated to producing a unique full-length musical that skewers and satirizes many dubious aspects of life at Columbia, the Varsity Show is written and performed exclusively by university undergraduates. Various renowned playwrights, composers, authors, directors, and actors have contributed to the Varsity Show, either as writers or performers, while students at Columbia, including [Richard Rodgers](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers "Richard Rodgers"), [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II"), [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart"), [Herman J. Mankiewicz](/wiki/Herman_J._Mankiewicz "Herman J. Mankiewicz"), [I. A. L. Diamond](/wiki/I._A._L._Diamond "I. A. L. Diamond"), [Herman Wouk](/wiki/Herman_Wouk "Herman Wouk"), [Greta Gerwig](/wiki/Greta_Gerwig "Greta Gerwig"), and [Kate McKinnon](/wiki/Kate_McKinnon "Kate McKinnon").[[295]](#cite_note-Vinciguerra-2021-302)

Notable past shows include *[Fly With Me](/wiki/Fly_With_Me_(musical) "Fly With Me (musical)")* (1920), [*The* *Streets of New York*](/wiki/The_Streets_of_New_York_(musical) "The Streets of New York (musical)") (1948), *The Sky's the Limit* (1954), and *Angels at Columbia* (1994). In particular, *Streets of New York*, after having been revived three times, opened [off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Broadway "Off-Broadway") in 1963 and was awarded a 1964 [Drama Desk Award](/wiki/Drama_Desk_Award "Drama Desk Award"). *The Mischief Maker* (1903), written by [Edgar Allan Woolf](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Woolf "Edgar Allan Woolf") and Cassius Freeborn, premiered at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden_(1890) "Madison Square Garden (1890)") in 1906 as *[Mam'zelle Champagne](/wiki/Mam%27zelle_Champagne "Mam'zelle Champagne")*.[[295]](#cite_note-Vinciguerra-2021-302)[[296]](#cite_note-303)

### Tree Lighting and Yule Log ceremonies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/College_Walk_night_jeh.JPG/250px-College_Walk_night_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:College_Walk_night_jeh.JPG)

Tree Lighting at College Walk

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Yule_Log_Ceremony_1910.png/250px-Yule_Log_Ceremony_1910.png)](/wiki/File:Yule_Log_Ceremony_1910.png)

The first modern Yule Log ceremony in [John Jay Hall](/wiki/John_Jay_Hall "John Jay Hall"), 1910

The campus Tree Lighting ceremony was inaugurated in 1998. It celebrates the illumination of the medium-sized trees lining College Walk in front of Kent Hall and [Hamilton Hall](/wiki/Hamilton_Hall_(Columbia_University) "Hamilton Hall (Columbia University)") on the east end and Dodge Hall and Pulitzer Hall on the west, just before finals week in early December. The lights remain on until February 28. Students meet at [the sundial](/wiki/Columbia_University_sundial "Columbia University sundial") for free hot chocolate, performances by *a cappella* groups, and speeches by the university president and a guest.[[297]](#cite_note-Hollander2010-304)

Immediately following the College Walk festivities is one of Columbia's older holiday traditions, the lighting of the Yule Log. The [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") ceremony dates to a period prior to the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), but lapsed before being revived by President [Nicholas Murray Butler](/wiki/Nicholas_Murray_Butler "Nicholas Murray Butler") in 1910. A troop of students dressed as [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") soldiers carry the eponymous log from the sundial to the lounge of [John Jay Hall](/wiki/John_Jay_Hall "John Jay Hall"), where it is lit amid the singing of seasonal carols. The Christmas ceremony is accompanied by a reading of *[A Visit From St. Nicholas](/wiki/A_Visit_From_St._Nicholas "A Visit From St. Nicholas")* by [Clement Clarke Moore](/wiki/Clement_Clarke_Moore "Clement Clarke Moore") and *[Yes, Virginia, There is a Santa Claus](/wiki/Yes,_Virginia,_There_is_a_Santa_Claus "Yes, Virginia, There is a Santa Claus")* by [Francis Pharcellus Church](/wiki/Francis_Pharcellus_Church "Francis Pharcellus Church").[[297]](#cite_note-Hollander2010-304)

See also: [List of Columbia University alumni and attendees](/wiki/List_of_Columbia_University_alumni_and_attendees "List of Columbia University alumni and attendees"); [List of Columbia University people in politics, military and law](/wiki/List_of_Columbia_University_people_in_politics,_military_and_law "List of Columbia University people in politics, military and law"); and [List of Nobel laureates affiliated with Columbia University as alumni or faculty](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_affiliated_with_Columbia_University_as_alumni_or_faculty "List of Nobel laureates affiliated with Columbia University as alumni or faculty")

The university has graduated many notable alumni, including five [Founding Fathers of the United States](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States"), [an author](/wiki/Gouverneur_Morris "Gouverneur Morris") of the United States Constitution and [a member](/wiki/Robert_R._Livingston_(chancellor) "Robert R. Livingston (chancellor)") of the [Committee of Five](/wiki/Committee_of_Five "Committee of Five"). Three United States presidents have attended Columbia,[[298]](#cite_note-parks2-305) as well as ten [justices of the Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Associate_Justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States"), including three [chief justices](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States"). As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Columbia_University&action=edit), 125 Pulitzer Prize winners and 39 Oscar winners have attended Columbia.[[299]](#cite_note-Columbia_Arts_Alumni2-306)

Columbia faculty received multiple Nobel Prizes, including Joseph Stiglitz (Economics, 2001), [Richard Axel](/wiki/Richard_Axel "Richard Axel") and [Linda Buck](/wiki/Linda_B._Buck "Linda B. Buck") (Medicine, 2004), [Edmund Phelps](/wiki/Edmund_Phelps "Edmund Phelps") (Economics, 2006), and [Orhan Pamuk](/wiki/Orhan_Pamuk "Orhan Pamuk") (Literature, 2006), among others.[[300]](#cite_note-307)[[301]](#cite_note-308)[[302]](#cite_note-309)[[303]](#cite_note-310)

In a 2016 ranking of universities worldwide with respect to living graduates who are billionaires, Columbia ranked second, after Harvard.[[304]](#cite_note-311)[[305]](#cite_note-Marie_Thibault2-312)

Former U.S. Presidents [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") and [Franklin Delano Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Delano_Roosevelt "Franklin Delano Roosevelt") attended the law school. Other political figures educated at Columbia include former U.S. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"),[[306]](#cite_note-313) Associate Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court [Ruth Bader Ginsburg](/wiki/Ruth_Bader_Ginsburg "Ruth Bader Ginsburg"),[[307]](#cite_note-314) former U.S. Secretary of State [Madeleine Albright](/wiki/Madeleine_Albright "Madeleine Albright"),[[308]](#cite_note-315) former chairman of the U.S. Federal Reserve Bank [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan"),[[309]](#cite_note-316) U.S. Attorney General [Eric Holder](/wiki/Eric_Holder "Eric Holder"), and U.S. Solicitor General [Donald Verrilli Jr.](/wiki/Donald_Verrilli_Jr. "Donald Verrilli Jr.")[[310]](#cite_note-317) The university has also educated 29 foreign [heads of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), including president of Georgia [Mikheil Saakashvili](/wiki/Mikheil_Saakashvili "Mikheil Saakashvili"), president of East Timor [José Ramos-Horta](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ramos-Horta "José Ramos-Horta"), president of Estonia [Toomas Hendrik Ilves](/wiki/Toomas_Hendrik_Ilves "Toomas Hendrik Ilves") and other historical figures such as [Wellington Koo](/wiki/Wellington_Koo "Wellington Koo"), [Radovan Karadžić](/wiki/Radovan_Karad%C5%BEi%C4%87 "Radovan Karadžić"), [Gaston Eyskens](/wiki/Gaston_Eyskens "Gaston Eyskens"), and [T. V. Soong](/wiki/T._V._Soong "T. V. Soong"). One of the founding fathers of modern India and the prime architect of the Constitution of India, [B. R. Ambedkar](/wiki/B._R._Ambedkar "B. R. Ambedkar"), was an alumnus.[[311]](#cite_note-318)[[312]](#cite_note-319)

Alumni of Columbia have occupied top positions in Wall Street and the rest of the business world. Notable members of the [Astor family](/wiki/Astor_family "Astor family")[[313]](#cite_note-320)[[314]](#cite_note-321) attended Columbia, while other business graduates include investor [Warren Buffett](/wiki/Warren_Buffett "Warren Buffett"),[[315]](#cite_note-322) former CEO of PBS and NBC [Lawrence K. Grossman](/wiki/Lawrence_K._Grossman "Lawrence K. Grossman"),[[316]](#cite_note-323) chairman of [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") [S. Robson Walton](/wiki/S._Robson_Walton "S. Robson Walton"),[[317]](#cite_note-324) [Bain Capital](/wiki/Bain_Capital "Bain Capital") Co-Managing Partner, [Jonathan Lavine](/wiki/Jonathan_Lavine "Jonathan Lavine"),[[318]](#cite_note-325)[[319]](#cite_note-326) [Thomson Reuters](/wiki/Thomson_Reuters "Thomson Reuters") CEO [Tom Glocer](/wiki/Tom_Glocer "Tom Glocer"),[[320]](#cite_note-327)[[321]](#cite_note-328) [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") president [Lynn Martin](/wiki/Lynn_Martin_(banker) "Lynn Martin (banker)"),[[322]](#cite_note-329) and [AllianceBernstein](/wiki/AllianceBernstein "AllianceBernstein") Chairman and CEO [Lewis A. Sanders](/wiki/Lewis_A._Sanders "Lewis A. Sanders").[[323]](#cite_note-330) CEOs of top Fortune 500 companies include [James P. Gorman](/wiki/James_P._Gorman "James P. Gorman") of [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley"),[[324]](#cite_note-331) [Robert J. Stevens](/wiki/Robert_J._Stevens "Robert J. Stevens") of [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin"),[[325]](#cite_note-332) [Philippe Dauman](/wiki/Philippe_Dauman "Philippe Dauman") of [Viacom](/wiki/Viacom_(2005%E2%80%932019) "Viacom (2005–2019)"),[[326]](#cite_note-333) [Robert Bakish](/wiki/Robert_Bakish "Robert Bakish") of [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global"),[[327]](#cite_note-334)[[328]](#cite_note-335) [Ursula Burns](/wiki/Ursula_Burns "Ursula Burns") of [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox"),[[329]](#cite_note-336) [Devin Wenig](/wiki/Devin_Wenig "Devin Wenig") of [EBay](/wiki/EBay "EBay"),[[330]](#cite_note-337) [Vikram Pandit](/wiki/Vikram_Pandit "Vikram Pandit") of [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup"),[[331]](#cite_note-338) [Ralph Izzo](/wiki/Ralph_Izzo "Ralph Izzo") of [Public Service Enterprise Group](/wiki/Public_Service_Enterprise_Group "Public Service Enterprise Group"),[[332]](#cite_note-339)[[333]](#cite_note-340) [Gail Koziara Boudreaux](/wiki/Gail_Koziara_Boudreaux "Gail Koziara Boudreaux") of [Anthem](/wiki/Anthem_(company) "Anthem (company)"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and [Frank Blake](/wiki/Frank_Blake_(businessman) "Frank Blake (businessman)") of [The Home Depot](/wiki/The_Home_Depot "The Home Depot").[[334]](#cite_note-341) Labor organizer and women's educator [Louise Leonard McLaren](/wiki/Louise_Leonard_McLaren "Louise Leonard McLaren") received her degree of Master of Arts from Columbia.[[335]](#cite_note-342)

In science and technology, Columbia alumni include: founder of [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM") [Herman Hollerith](/wiki/Herman_Hollerith "Herman Hollerith");[[336]](#cite_note-343) inventor of [FM radio](/wiki/FM_broadcast "FM broadcast") [Edwin Armstrong](/wiki/Edwin_Armstrong "Edwin Armstrong");[[337]](#cite_note-344) [Francis Mechner](/wiki/Francis_Mechner "Francis Mechner"); integral in development of the [nuclear submarine](/wiki/Nuclear_submarine "Nuclear submarine") [Hyman Rickover](/wiki/Hyman_G._Rickover "Hyman G. Rickover");[[338]](#cite_note-345) founder of [Google China](/wiki/Google_China "Google China") [Kai-Fu Lee](/wiki/Kai-Fu_Lee "Kai-Fu Lee");[[339]](#cite_note-346) scientists [Stephen Jay Gould](/wiki/Stephen_Jay_Gould "Stephen Jay Gould"),[[340]](#cite_note-347) [Robert Millikan](/wiki/Robert_Millikan "Robert Millikan"),[[341]](#cite_note-348) [Helium–neon laser](/wiki/Helium%E2%80%93neon_laser "Helium–neon laser") inventor [Ali Javan](/wiki/Ali_Javan "Ali Javan") and [Mihajlo Pupin](/wiki/Mihajlo_Pupin "Mihajlo Pupin");[[342]](#cite_note-349) chief-engineer of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"), [William Barclay Parsons](/wiki/William_Barclay_Parsons "William Barclay Parsons");[[343]](#cite_note-350) philosophers [Irwin Edman](/wiki/Irwin_Edman "Irwin Edman")[[344]](#cite_note-Edman2011-351) and [Robert Nozick](/wiki/Robert_Nozick "Robert Nozick");[[344]](#cite_note-Edman2011-351) economist [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman");[[345]](#cite_note-352) psychologist [Harriet Babcock](/wiki/Harriet_Babcock "Harriet Babcock");[[346]](#cite_note-353) archaeologist [Josephine Platner Shear](/wiki/Josephine_Platner_Shear "Josephine Platner Shear");[[347]](#cite_note-Princeton-1936-354) biologist and medical researcher [Leona Zacharias](/wiki/Leona_Zacharias "Leona Zacharias"),[[348]](#cite_note-355) and sociologists [Lewis A. Coser](/wiki/Lewis_A._Coser "Lewis A. Coser") and [Rose Laub Coser](/wiki/Rose_Laub_Coser "Rose Laub Coser").[[349]](#cite_note-356)[[350]](#cite_note-357)

Many Columbia alumni have gone on to renowned careers in the arts, including composers [Richard Rodgers](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers "Richard Rodgers"),[[351]](#cite_note-358) [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II"),[[352]](#cite_note-359) [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart"),[[353]](#cite_note-360) and [Art Garfunkel](/wiki/Art_Garfunkel "Art Garfunkel");[[354]](#cite_note-361) and painter [Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe").[[355]](#cite_note-362) Five United States [Poet Laureates](/wiki/Poet_Laureate "Poet Laureate") received their degrees from Columbia. Columbia alumni have made an indelible mark in the field of American poetry and literature, with such people as [Jack Kerouac](/wiki/Jack_Kerouac "Jack Kerouac") and [Allen Ginsberg](/wiki/Allen_Ginsberg "Allen Ginsberg"), pioneers of the [Beat Generation](/wiki/Beat_Generation "Beat Generation");[[356]](#cite_note-363) and [Langston Hughes](/wiki/Langston_Hughes "Langston Hughes") and [Zora Neale Hurston](/wiki/Zora_Neale_Hurston "Zora Neale Hurston"), seminal figures in the [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance"),[[357]](#cite_note-364)[[358]](#cite_note-365) all having attended the university. Other notable writers who attended Columbia include authors [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov"),[[359]](#cite_note-366) [J.D. Salinger](/wiki/J.D._Salinger "J.D. Salinger"),[[360]](#cite_note-367) [Upton Sinclair](/wiki/Upton_Sinclair "Upton Sinclair"),[[361]](#cite_note-368) [Ursula K. Le Guin](/wiki/Ursula_K._Le_Guin "Ursula K. Le Guin"),[[362]](#cite_note-369) [Danielle Valore Evans](/wiki/Danielle_Valore_Evans "Danielle Valore Evans"),[[363]](#cite_note-370) and [Hunter S. Thompson](/wiki/Hunter_S._Thompson "Hunter S. Thompson").[[364]](#cite_note-371) In architecture, [William Lee Stoddart](/wiki/William_Lee_Stoddart "William Lee Stoddart"), a prolific architect of [U.S. East Coast](/wiki/Eastern_United_States "Eastern United States") hotels, is an alumnus.[[365]](#cite_note-NYT-Obituary-372)

University alumni have also been very prominent in the film industry, with 33 alumni and former students winning a combined 43 [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") (as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Columbia_University&action=edit)).[[299]](#cite_note-Columbia_Arts_Alumni2-306) Some notable Columbia alumni that have gone on to work in film include directors [Sidney Lumet](/wiki/Sidney_Lumet "Sidney Lumet") (*[12 Angry Men](/wiki/12_Angry_Men_(1957_film) "12 Angry Men (1957 film)")*)[[366]](#cite_note-373) and [Kathryn Bigelow](/wiki/Kathryn_Bigelow "Kathryn Bigelow") (*[The Hurt Locker](/wiki/The_Hurt_Locker "The Hurt Locker")*),[[367]](#cite_note-374) screenwriters [Howard Koch](/wiki/Howard_Koch_(screenwriter) "Howard Koch (screenwriter)") (*[Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca_(film) "Casablanca (film)")*)[[368]](#cite_note-375) and [Joseph L. Mankiewicz](/wiki/Joseph_L._Mankiewicz "Joseph L. Mankiewicz") (*[All About Eve](/wiki/All_About_Eve "All About Eve")*),[[369]](#cite_note-376) and actors [James Cagney](/wiki/James_Cagney "James Cagney"),[[370]](#cite_note-377) [Ed Harris](/wiki/Ed_Harris "Ed Harris") and [Timothée Chalamet](/wiki/Timoth%C3%A9e_Chalamet "Timothée Chalamet").[[371]](#cite_note-378)

### Faculty

Columbia University's faculty has included many notable names in the role of university [president](/wiki/President_of_Columbia_University "President of Columbia University"), including [34th](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of presidents of the United States") [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower"), [4th](/wiki/List_of_vice_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of vice presidents of the United States") [Vice President of the United States](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") [George Clinton](/wiki/George_Clinton_(vice_president) "George Clinton (vice president)"), [Founding Father](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States") and [U.S. Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") from Connecticut [William Samuel Johnson](/wiki/William_Samuel_Johnson "William Samuel Johnson"), [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") laureate [Nicholas Murray Butler](/wiki/Nicholas_Murray_Butler "Nicholas Murray Butler"), and [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") scholar [Lee Bollinger](/wiki/Lee_Bollinger "Lee Bollinger").[[26]](#cite_note-Columbia_U-2021b-26)

Other notable Columbia University faculty include [Zbigniew Brzezinski](/wiki/Zbigniew_Brzezinski "Zbigniew Brzezinski"), [Sonia Sotomayor](/wiki/Sonia_Sotomayor "Sonia Sotomayor"), [Kimberlé Crenshaw](/wiki/Kimberl%C3%A9_Crenshaw "Kimberlé Crenshaw"), [Lee Bollinger](/wiki/Lee_Bollinger "Lee Bollinger"), [Franz Boas](/wiki/Franz_Boas "Franz Boas"), [Margaret Mead](/wiki/Margaret_Mead "Margaret Mead"), [Edward Sapir](/wiki/Edward_Sapir "Edward Sapir"), [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey"), [Charles A. Beard](/wiki/Charles_A._Beard "Charles A. Beard"), [Max Horkheimer](/wiki/Max_Horkheimer "Max Horkheimer"), [Herbert Marcuse](/wiki/Herbert_Marcuse "Herbert Marcuse"), [Edward Said](/wiki/Edward_Said "Edward Said"), [Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak](/wiki/Gayatri_Chakravorty_Spivak "Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak"), [Orhan Pamuk](/wiki/Orhan_Pamuk "Orhan Pamuk"), [Edwin Howard Armstrong](/wiki/Edwin_Howard_Armstrong "Edwin Howard Armstrong"), [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi"), [Chien-Shiung Wu](/wiki/Chien-Shiung_Wu "Chien-Shiung Wu"), [Tsung-Dao Lee](/wiki/Tsung-Dao_Lee "Tsung-Dao Lee"), [Jack Steinberger](/wiki/Jack_Steinberger "Jack Steinberger"), [Joachim Frank](/wiki/Joachim_Frank "Joachim Frank"), [Joseph Stiglitz](/wiki/Joseph_Stiglitz "Joseph Stiglitz"), [Jeffrey Sachs](/wiki/Jeffrey_Sachs "Jeffrey Sachs"), [Robert Mundell](/wiki/Robert_Mundell "Robert Mundell"), [Thomas Hunt Morgan](/wiki/Thomas_Hunt_Morgan "Thomas Hunt Morgan"), [Eric Kandel](/wiki/Eric_Kandel "Eric Kandel"), [Richard Axel](/wiki/Richard_Axel "Richard Axel"), [Andrei Okounkov](/wiki/Andrei_Okounkov "Andrei Okounkov"), [Minky Worden](/wiki/Minky_Worden "Minky Worden"), and [William York Tindall](/wiki/William_York_Tindall "William York Tindall").[[372]](#cite_note-379)

As of 2021, Columbia employs 4,381 faculty, including 70 members of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences"),[[373]](#cite_note-380) 178 members of the [American Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences"),[[374]](#cite_note-381) and 65 members of the [National Academy of Medicine](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Medicine "National Academy of Medicine").[[375]](#cite_note-382) In total, the Columbia faculty has included 52 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"), 12 [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") recipients,[[376]](#cite_note-383) and 32 [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering") members.[[377]](#cite_note-384)

Columbia University faculty played particularly important roles during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and the creation of the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") under President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"), who attended Columbia Law School. The three core members of Roosevelt's [Brain Trust](/wiki/Brain_trust#Roosevelt's_"Brain_Trust" "Brain trust"): [Adolf A. Berle](/wiki/Adolf_A._Berle "Adolf A. Berle"), [Raymond Moley](/wiki/Raymond_Moley "Raymond Moley"), and [Rexford Tugwell](/wiki/Rexford_Tugwell "Rexford Tugwell"), were law professors at Columbia.[[378]](#cite_note-385) Former United States Secretary of Labor, Francis Perkins was the principle architect of the New Deal and also earned her Master's from Columbia University.[[379]](#cite_note-386) The [Statistical Research Group](/wiki/The_Statistical_Research_Group_of_World_War_II "The Statistical Research Group of World War II"), which used statistics to analyze military problems during World War II, was composed of Columbia researchers and faculty including [George Stigler](/wiki/George_Stigler "George Stigler") and [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman").[[380]](#cite_note-387) Columbia faculty and researchers, including [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi"), [Leo Szilard](/wiki/Leo_Szilard "Leo Szilard"), [Eugene T. Booth](/wiki/Eugene_T._Booth "Eugene T. Booth"), [John R. Dunning](/wiki/John_R._Dunning "John R. Dunning"), [George B. Pegram](/wiki/George_B._Pegram "George B. Pegram"), [Walter Zinn](/wiki/Walter_Zinn "Walter Zinn"), [Chien-Shiung Wu](/wiki/Chien-Shiung_Wu "Chien-Shiung Wu"), [Francis G. Slack](/wiki/Francis_G._Slack "Francis G. Slack"), [Harold Urey](/wiki/Harold_Urey "Harold Urey"), [Herbert L. Anderson](/wiki/Herbert_L._Anderson "Herbert L. Anderson"), and [Isidor Isaac Rabi](/wiki/Isidor_Isaac_Rabi "Isidor Isaac Rabi"), also played a significant role during the early phases of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project").[[381]](#cite_note-388)

Following the rise of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), the exiled [Institute for Social Research](/wiki/University_of_Frankfurt_Institute_for_Social_Research "University of Frankfurt Institute for Social Research") at [Goethe University Frankfurt](/wiki/Goethe_University_Frankfurt "Goethe University Frankfurt") would affiliate itself with Columbia from 1934 to 1950.[[382]](#cite_note-389) It was during this period that thinkers including [Theodor W. Adorno](/wiki/Theodor_W._Adorno "Theodor W. Adorno"), [Max Horkheimer](/wiki/Max_Horkheimer "Max Horkheimer"), and [Herbert Marcuse](/wiki/Herbert_Marcuse "Herbert Marcuse") wrote and published some of the most seminal works of the [Frankfurt School](/wiki/Frankfurt_School "Frankfurt School"), including *[Reason and Revolution](/wiki/Reason_and_Revolution "Reason and Revolution")*, *[Dialectic of Enlightenment](/wiki/Dialectic_of_Enlightenment "Dialectic of Enlightenment")*, and [*Eclipse of Reason*](/wiki/Eclipse_of_Reason_(Horkheimer) "Eclipse of Reason (Horkheimer)").[[383]](#cite_note-390) Professors [Edward Said](/wiki/Edward_Said "Edward Said"), author of [*Orientalism*](/wiki/Orientalism_(book) "Orientalism (book)"), and [Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak](/wiki/Gayatri_Chakravorty_Spivak "Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak") are generally considered as founders of the field of [postcolonialism](/wiki/Postcolonialism "Postcolonialism");[[384]](#cite_note-391)[[385]](#cite_note-392) other professors that have significantly contributed to the field include [Hamid Dabashi](/wiki/Hamid_Dabashi "Hamid Dabashi"), [Rashid Khalidi](/wiki/Rashid_Khalidi "Rashid Khalidi"), and [Joseph Massad](/wiki/Joseph_Massad "Joseph Massad").[[386]](#cite_note-393)[[387]](#cite_note-394)[[388]](#cite_note-395) The works of professors [Kimberlé Crenshaw](/wiki/Kimberl%C3%A9_Crenshaw "Kimberlé Crenshaw"), [Patricia J. Williams](/wiki/Patricia_J._Williams "Patricia J. Williams"), and [Kendall Thomas](/wiki/Kendall_Thomas "Kendall Thomas") were foundational to the field of [critical race theory](/wiki/Critical_race_theory "Critical race theory").[[389]](#cite_note-396)

Columbia and its affiliated faculty have also made significant contributions to the study of religion. The affiliated Union Theological Seminary is a center of [liberal Christianity](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") in the United States, having served as the birthplace of [Black theology](/wiki/Black_theology "Black theology") through the efforts of faculty including [James H. Cone](/wiki/James_H._Cone "James H. Cone") and [Cornel West](/wiki/Cornel_West "Cornel West"),[[390]](#cite_note-397)[[391]](#cite_note-398) and [Womanist theology](/wiki/Womanist_theology "Womanist theology"), through the works of [Katie Cannon](/wiki/Katie_Cannon "Katie Cannon"), [Emilie Townes](/wiki/Emilie_Townes "Emilie Townes"), and [Delores S. Williams](/wiki/Delores_S._Williams "Delores S. Williams").[[392]](#cite_note-399)[[393]](#cite_note-400)[[394]](#cite_note-401) Likewise, the Jewish Theological Seminary of America was the birthplace of [Conservative Judaism](/wiki/Conservative_Judaism "Conservative Judaism") movement in the United States, which was founded and led by faculty members including [Solomon Schechter](/wiki/Solomon_Schechter "Solomon Schechter"), [Alexander Kohut](/wiki/Alexander_Kohut "Alexander Kohut"), and [Louis Ginzberg](/wiki/Louis_Ginzberg "Louis Ginzberg") in the early 20th century, and is a major center for Jewish studies in general.[[395]](#cite_note-402)

Other schools of thought in the humanities to which Columbia professors made significant contributions include the [Dunning School](/wiki/Dunning_School "Dunning School"), founded by [William Archibald Dunning](/wiki/William_Archibald_Dunning "William Archibald Dunning");[[396]](#cite_note-403)[[397]](#cite_note-SmithLoweryxi2-404) the anthropological schools of [historical particularism](/wiki/Historical_particularism "Historical particularism") and [cultural relativism](/wiki/Cultural_relativism "Cultural relativism"), founded by [Franz Boas](/wiki/Franz_Boas "Franz Boas");[[398]](#cite_note-405) and [functional psychology](/wiki/Functional_psychology "Functional psychology"), whose founders and proponents include [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey"), [James McKeen Cattell](/wiki/James_McKeen_Cattell "James McKeen Cattell"), [Edward L. Thorndike](/wiki/Edward_L._Thorndike "Edward L. Thorndike"), and [Robert S. Woodworth](/wiki/Robert_S._Woodworth "Robert S. Woodworth").[[399]](#cite_note-406)

* [![icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Diploma_icon.png/20px-Diploma_icon.png)](/wiki/File:Diploma_icon.png) [Education](/wiki/Portal:Education "Portal:Education")

* *[Columbia Encyclopedia](/wiki/Columbia_Encyclopedia "Columbia Encyclopedia")*
* [Columbia Glacier](/wiki/Columbia_Glacier_(Alaska) "Columbia Glacier (Alaska)"), a [glacier](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), U.S., named for Columbia University
* [Columbia MM](/wiki/Columbia_MM "Columbia MM"), a text-based mail client developed at Columbia University
* [Columbia Non-neutral Torus](/wiki/Columbia_Non-neutral_Torus "Columbia Non-neutral Torus"), a small stellarator at the Columbia University Plasma Physics Laboratory
* *[Columbia-Princeton Electronic Music Center](/wiki/Columbia-Princeton_Electronic_Music_Center_(album) "Columbia-Princeton Electronic Music Center (album)")*, an album of electronic music released in 1961
* *[Columbia Revolt](/wiki/Columbia_Revolt "Columbia Revolt")*, a black-and-white 1968 documentary film
* [Columbia Scholastic Press Association](/wiki/Columbia_Scholastic_Press_Association "Columbia Scholastic Press Association")
* [Columbia School of Linguistics](/wiki/Columbia_School_of_Linguistics "Columbia School of Linguistics")
* [Columbia Spelling Board](/wiki/Columbia_Spelling_Board "Columbia Spelling Board"), a historic etymological organization
* [Columbia Unbecoming controversy](/wiki/Columbia_Unbecoming_controversy "Columbia Unbecoming controversy")
* [Columbia University in popular culture](/wiki/Columbia_University_in_popular_culture "Columbia University in popular culture")
* [Mount Columbia](/wiki/Mount_Columbia_(Colorado) "Mount Columbia (Colorado)"), a mountain in [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), U.S., named for Columbia University
* [Nutellagate](/wiki/Nutellagate "Nutellagate"), a controversy surrounding high [Nutella](/wiki/Nutella "Nutella") consumption at Columbia University
* *[The Strawberry Statement](/wiki/The_Strawberry_Statement "The Strawberry Statement")*, a non-fiction account of the 1968 protests
* [2024 Columbia University pro-Palestinian campus occupations](/wiki/2024_Columbia_University_pro-Palestinian_campus_occupations "2024 Columbia University pro-Palestinian campus occupations")

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** Founding Fathers include five alumni: [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) [John Jay](/wiki/John_Jay "John Jay"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Robert R. Livingston](/wiki/Robert_Livingston_(1746%E2%80%931813) "Robert Livingston (1746–1813)"),[[22]](#cite_note-22) [Egbert Benson](/wiki/Egbert_Benson "Egbert Benson"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) and [Gouverneur Morris](/wiki/Gouverneur_Morris "Gouverneur Morris").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Additionally, Founding Fathers [George Clinton](/wiki/George_Clinton_(vice_president) "George Clinton (vice president)")[[25]](#cite_note-25) and [William Samuel Johnson](/wiki/William_Samuel_Johnson "William Samuel Johnson")[[26]](#cite_note-Columbia_U-2021b-26) served as [presidents of the university](/wiki/President_of_Columbia_University "President of Columbia University").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** Three presidents have attended Columbia: [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"), and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"). [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") served as the president of the university from 1948 to 1953.
